text.text: ().

### Prerequisites

The game on this page aims to reinforce knowledge of indigenous numerical systems and the numbers we use today. Before starting, we suggest studying the following previous pages:
- **Our sides are equal**: in this first text, we show for people their body symmetry (<htcite0>).
- **Couting**: we introduce the idea of counting using our body (<htcite1>).
- **Beyond fingers**: exploring the limitations and the necessity of new tools to count (<htcite2>).
- **Building to Count (Soroban, 算盤)**: In this text, we learn how Japan (Nippon-koku or Nihon-koku, 日本国) built its abacus and how to use it to count (<htcite3>).
- **Building to Count (Suanpan, 算盤)**: In this text, we learn about the abacus built in China (Zhōngguó, 中国) and how to represent numbers on it (<htcite4>).
- **Translation Game (Part I)**: First phase of this game, which trains numbers between 1 and 9 (<htcite5>).
- **Number Families (Part I)**: In this text, we begin to study numbers with more than one digit and learn that they can be presented in families (<htcite6>).
- **Number Families (Part II)**: In this text, we learn the names of other number families with more than two digits (<htcite7>).
- **Higher Orders with Soroban and Suanpan (算盤)**: In this text, we learn how to represent on the abacuses the numerical orders studied in previous texts (<htcite8>).

source.page: Higher orders with Soroban and Suanpan (算盤)

text.text:
### Introduction

Just as we did in the first level of the game (<htcite0>), our goal here is to reinforce our knowledge of numerical representation, but this time we will do it with numbers of more than one digit.

Although the way we represent numbers on two of the abacuses — Soroban and Suanpan (算盤) — (<htcite1>; <htcite2>; <htcite3>) is not exactly the same as that used in the *Inca Yupana* or even in the Mesoamerican system (<htcite4>; <htcite5>), the logic of the numerical sequence is the same in any of these systems.

Thus, in this second level, we will first use the abacuses of China (Zhongguo 中國) and Japan (Nippon koku o Nihon koku, 日本国), and then the numerical systems of *Abya Yala* (América).

source.page: Higher orders with Soroban and Suanpan (算盤)

source.text: Casares Contreras, Orlando & Cetina, José & Cabrera, Venus Sarahí
Casares Contreras, Orlando & Cetina, José & Cabrera, Venus Sarahí

text.text: ### Translating to Asia #### The Game (Objective and Rules) Our objective will be to represent the Indo-Arabic number in a version of the abacus (Soroban (算盤) or Suanpan (算盤)). At this stage, we will not yet move the decimal marker (⚫). At the top left, a numerical value between 10 and 999,999,999 is displayed above the abacus. Move the lower beads (which have a value of 1) and the upper beads (which have a value of 5) until the “Value” field of the abacus shows the same number displayed at the top. When this happens, the message will appear: Congratulations!! To start a new exercise, simply click the **Reset** button. **TIP**: Just as we are born as babies and grow through the stages of childhood, adolescence, and adulthood (), numbers must be written in stages, starting from the column where our decimal marker is located, which represents our baby stage. After making the first number on the far right equal to the first number shown, go to the immediately adjacent column and repeat the same process. After all, to grow, we also repeat stages, regardless of the life stage we are in.

### Translating numbers from *Abya Ayala* (America ) #### Limits The next application, like the previous ones on this page, has artificial limitations that we are adding. However, this does not mean that we cannot represent larger numbers in it. For the study of numerical systems and tools of *Abya Yala* (America), we will present the Mesoamerican system in the first stage. Next, we will continue with the Yupana, using the *Yupana Inka Tawa Pukllay* method with a maximum of two lines, which represent the units and tens controlled by arrows (; ). #### Reminder To read a number in the Yupana, it is necessary to observe the four quadrants. In contrast, Mesoamerican numbers are represented in a single quadrant. Furthermore, when comparing the indigenous representations of *Abya Yala* (America) with other Asian systems (abacus and Indo-Arabic), we observe that in Asian systems numbers grow from right to left, while in indigenous systems they grow from bottom to top, just like in our own system. To make this clearer, we color units yellow and tens blue, in order to explain how numbers are being translated. #### Objective and rules Translate the Indo-Arabic numerals that appear into Mesoamerican format or into the Yupana. For each translated value, you will receive a medal (). When you complete the stages, you will receive 3 medals (). To move to the next calculation, click on .
Translate the first indigenous numbers

We use the Yupana through the "Yupana Inka Tawa Pukllay" method, developed and published by Dhavit Prem () of the Yupanki Association ().
The application of Mesoamerican numbers is based on the work of Orlando Josué Casares Contreras, José M. Gamboa Cetina, and Venus Sarahí Estrella Cabrera ( ; ). Additionally, we incorporate data from the works of Francisco Barriga Puente and Ximena Catepillán ( ; ).
Finally, we employ various counting methods inspired by the work of Francisco A. González Redondo et al. ().
